1:
"Therefore we ought to give the more earnest heed to the things which we have heard, lest at any time we should let them slip." The witnesses note the first warning: heed the gospel lest we drift from it. On "we ought to give the more earnest heed to the things which we have heard," the grammatical voices note the inference. Barnes and Robertson note the practical application of chapter 1 ("therefore," because the Son is so great): "we ought to give the more earnest heed (prosechein — attend more closely, pay greater attention) to the things which we have heard (the gospel)" — the surpassing dignity of the Son makes the more imperative a close attention to his message.

On "lest at any time we should let them slip," the witnesses note the danger. Barnes and Calvin note the figure: "lest at any time we should let them slip (pararyomen — drift away, flow past, slip away, as a ship that drifts past the harbour, or water that runs out of a leaky vessel)" — the danger is not chiefly violent apostasy but a gradual, careless drifting away from the truth.

The puritan Henry notes the peril of neglect: the great danger is inattention; truths heard but not heeded slip away, and the soul, like a drifting ship, is carried past its anchorage; carelessness, not only open rejection, is fatal. The verse displays the first warning: heed the gospel lest we drift.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inference — "we ought to give the more earnest heed (attend more closely) to the things which we have heard," the Son's dignity demanding close attention to his message (Barnes, Robertson); "lest we should let them slip (drift away, flow past)," the danger of gradual careless drifting, like a ship past the harbour (Barnes, Calvin); the peril of neglect, truths slipping away through inattention (Henry). The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the duty: earnest heed. The verse displays the things: heard. The verse displays the danger: let them slip. The verse displays the figure: drifting away. The warning displays the soul urged to heed lest it drift. The verse displays the heed: earnest. The verse displays the gospel: heard. The verse displays the slipping: feared. The first warning — heed the gospel lest we drift from it — displays the practical force of the Son's supremacy: because he is so great, we ought to attend the more closely to the message we have heard, lest we gradually drift away and let it slip past us like a ship carried past its harbour. The verse displays the warning against drifting, grounded in the law's sure penalty (v.2).
2:
"For if the word spoken by angels was stedfast, and every transgression and disobedience received a just recompence of reward;" The brief verse notes the law spoken by angels, which punished every transgression. The grammatical voices note the argument from the less to the greater. Barnes and Robertson note the premise of the warning: "if the word spoken by angels (the law, given through the mediation of angels, cf. Acts 7:53; Galatians 3:19) was stedfast (firm, binding, valid)" — the law, mediated by angels, was firm and authoritative.

On "every transgression and disobedience received a just recompence," the witnesses note the law's sanctions. Barnes and Calvin note that under the law "every transgression (overstepping) and disobedience (refusal to hear) received a just recompence (endikon misthapodosian — a just penalty, a deserved punishment)" — the law punished every violation justly; its penalties were sure and exact.

The grammatical-historical note the a fortiori force: if even the law, given through angels (inferior to the Son), was so binding that every breach was punished, how much more binding is the gospel spoken by the Son himself — setting up the argument of v.3. The verse displays the law spoken by angels, which punished every transgression.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the premise — "if the word spoken by angels (the law, mediated by angels) was stedfast (firm, binding)," the law authoritative (Barnes, Robertson); "every transgression and disobedience received a just recompence (deserved penalty)," the law punishing every breach justly (Barnes, Calvin); the a fortiori force, the law through angels being so binding, how much more the gospel by the Son (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the law's sanctions. The verse marks the law: spoken by angels. The verse displays the firmness: stedfast. The verse displays the penalty: just recompence. The verse displays the argument: from less to greater. The premise displays the law binding and every breach punished. The verse displays the word: by angels. The verse displays the transgression: punished. The verse displays the recompence: just. The law spoken by angels, which punished every transgression, displays the ground of the warning's argument: if even the law, given through angels, was firm and binding so that every violation met its just penalty, then far more binding is the gospel spoken by the Son — and far graver the danger of neglecting it. The verse displays the law's sure penalty, magnifying the peril of neglecting the gospel (v.3).
3:
"How shall we escape, if we neglect so great salvation; which at the first began to be spoken by the Lord, and was confirmed unto us by them that heard him;" The witnesses note the peril of neglecting so great a salvation, spoken by the Lord. On "how shall we escape, if we neglect so great salvation," the grammatical voices note the searching question. Barnes and Robertson note the climax of the warning: "how shall we escape (the just penalty) if we neglect (amelesantes — disregard, make light of, are careless of) so great salvation?" — the rhetorical question has no answer; there is no escape for those who neglect the gospel; "neglect" — not violent rejection but mere careless disregard — is itself fatal.

On "so great salvation," the witnesses note its greatness. Barnes and Calvin note the emphasis: the salvation is "so great" — surpassing the law in its author (the Son), its blessings, and its cost; the greater the salvation, the graver the guilt of neglecting it.

On "began to be spoken by the Lord, and was confirmed unto us by them that heard him," the grammatical-historical note its sure transmission: this salvation "at the first began to be spoken by the Lord (the Lord Jesus himself), and was confirmed (made sure, guaranteed) unto us by them that heard him (the apostles, eyewitnesses)" — first proclaimed by the Lord, then surely transmitted by those who heard him; an unimpeachable witness. The verse displays the peril of neglecting so great a salvation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the searching question — "how shall we escape if we neglect (disregard, make light of) so great salvation?", no escape for the careless, neglect itself fatal (Barnes, Robertson); the salvation "so great," surpassing the law in author, blessings, and cost, the greater the salvation the graver the neglect (Barnes, Calvin); "began to be spoken by the Lord, confirmed by them that heard him," proclaimed by the Lord and surely transmitted by the eyewitnesses (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the great salvation. The verse marks the question: how shall we escape. The verse displays the sin: neglect. The verse displays the greatness: so great salvation. The verse displays the witness: the Lord and his hearers. The warning displays no escape for those who neglect the gospel. The verse displays the escape: none. The verse displays the salvation: great. The verse displays the speaker: the Lord. The peril of neglecting so great a salvation, spoken by the Lord, displays the climax of the warning: if the law through angels punished every breach, how shall we escape if we neglect the far greater salvation — first spoken by the Lord himself and confirmed by those who heard him? Neglect, not only rejection, leaves no escape. The verse displays the great salvation, attested by God's own witness (v.4).
4:
"God also bearing them witness, both with signs and wonders, and with divers miracles, and gifts of the Holy Ghost, according to his own will?" The brief verse notes God's confirming witness to the gospel by signs and gifts. The grammatical voices note the divine attestation. Barnes and Robertson note that God added his own testimony to that of the eyewitnesses (v.3): "God also bearing them witness (synepimartyrountos — joining his testimony, attesting alongside), both with signs (semeiois — significant tokens) and wonders (terasin — marvels) and with divers miracles (poikilais dynamesin — various mighty works)" — God confirmed the gospel by a threefold display of supernatural power.

On the threefold attestation, the witnesses note their force. Barnes and Calvin note the cumulative testimony: signs, wonders, and miracles together attested that the message was from God; the supernatural works authenticated the apostolic witness, leaving the hearers without excuse.

On "gifts of the Holy Ghost, according to his own will," the grammatical-historical note the further witness: "gifts (distributions, apportionings) of the Holy Ghost, according to his own (God's) will" — the charismatic gifts of the Spirit, distributed as God willed, were a further divine attestation; God confirmed the gospel both by miracles and by the Spirit's gifts. The verse displays God's confirming witness to the gospel.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the divine attestation — "God also bearing them witness (joining his testimony), with signs (tokens) and wonders (marvels) and divers miracles (mighty works)," God confirming the gospel by supernatural power (Barnes, Robertson); the cumulative testimony authenticating the apostolic witness, leaving none excused (Barnes, Calvin); "gifts of the Holy Ghost, according to his own will," the Spirit's gifts distributed as God willed, a further attestation (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the divine witness. The verse marks the witness: God bearing it. The verse displays the means: signs, wonders, miracles. The verse displays the gifts: of the Holy Ghost. The verse displays the will: God's own. The attestation displays God confirming the gospel supernaturally. The verse displays the signs: given. The verse displays the wonders: wrought. The verse displays the gifts: distributed. God's confirming witness to the gospel by signs and gifts displays the unimpeachable testimony behind the great salvation: God himself attested the message of the Lord and the apostles by signs, wonders, miracles, and the gifts of the Holy Ghost distributed as he willed — so that to neglect it is to despise God's own witness. The verse displays the divine attestation, closing the warning and resuming the great theme of the Son made man (vv.5-9).
5:
"For unto the angels hath he not put in subjection the world to come, whereof we speak." The brief verse notes the world to come subjected not to angels but to the Son. The grammatical voices note the resumption of the argument. Barnes and Robertson note the return to the theme of the Son's superiority over angels: "for unto the angels hath he not put in subjection (hypetaxen — subjected, made subordinate) the world to come (the future order, the Messianic age and its kingdom)" — the coming world is not given to the rule of angels.

On "the world to come," the witnesses note its meaning. Barnes and Calvin note that "the world to come" is the new order inaugurated by Christ — the Messianic dispensation, the age of the gospel and the kingdom of the Son; this is not subjected to angels but to the Son and (in him) to redeemed man.

On "whereof we speak," the eastern Chrysostom and puritan Henry note the connection: "whereof we speak" — the very subject of the epistle, the new dispensation under Christ; the writer turns to show that this coming world is subjected not to angels but to man, in the person of the Son (vv.6-9). The verse displays the world to come subjected not to angels but to the Son.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the resumption — "unto the angels hath he not put in subjection (subjected) the world to come (the Messianic age and kingdom)," the coming world not given to angels' rule (Barnes, Robertson); "the world to come" the new order inaugurated by Christ, the gospel age and the Son's kingdom (Barnes, Calvin); "whereof we speak," the subject of the epistle, subjected to man in the Son (Chrysostom, Henry). The verse displays the world to come. The verse marks the subjection: not to angels. The verse displays the realm: the world to come. The verse displays the meaning: the Messianic age. The verse displays the connection: whereof we speak. The subjection displays the coming world given not to angels but the Son. The verse displays the angels: not ruling. The verse displays the world: to come. The verse displays the subject: the new order. The world to come subjected not to angels but to the Son displays the renewed argument: the new order inaugurated by Christ — the Messianic age and kingdom of which the epistle speaks — is not put under the rule of angels, but under man, in the person of the Son. The verse displays the kingdom not given to angels, proved from Psalm 8 (vv.6-8).
6:
"But one in a certain place testified, saying, What is man, that thou art mindful of him? or the son of man, that thou visitest him?" The brief verse notes the psalmist's wonder that God is mindful of man. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note the quotation of Psalm 8:4-6: "one in a certain place testified (the manner of citation common to the writer, who names neither author nor place, the source being well known)" — the writer cites the Psalm to prove that dominion over the coming world is given to man, not angels.

On "what is man, that thou art mindful of him?", the witnesses note the wonder. Barnes and Calvin note the psalmist's astonishment: "what is man (enosh — frail man), that thou art mindful (rememberest, carest for) him? or the son of man (the descendant of Adam), that thou visitest (lookest upon, regardest with favour) him?" — the wonder that the great God should so regard frail, lowly man.

On the application, the grammatical-historical note the double reference: the Psalm speaks first of man in general (the dignity God gave Adam), but the writer will apply it pre-eminently to the Son of Man, Christ, in whom man's destiny is fulfilled (v.9). The verse displays the psalmist's wonder that God is mindful of man.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the citation — "one in a certain place testified" (Psalm 8:4-6), cited to prove dominion given to man not angels (Barnes, Robertson); the wonder "what is man, that thou art mindful of him? or the son of man, that thou visitest him?", astonishment that God regards frail, lowly man (Barnes, Calvin); the double reference, man in general but pre-eminently the Son of Man Christ (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the wonder. The verse marks the citation: Psalm 8. The verse displays the question: what is man. The verse displays the wonder: God mindful of him. The verse displays the application: man, and the Son of Man. The citation displays the wonder of God's regard for man. The verse displays the man: frail. The verse displays the mindfulness: God's. The verse displays the visiting: in favour. The psalmist's wonder that God is mindful of man displays the text the writer applies to Christ: the Psalm marvels that the great God should be mindful of frail man and visit the son of man — a wonder fulfilled pre-eminently in the Son of Man, Christ, in whom man's high destiny is realized. The verse displays the dignity given to man, detailed in his dominion (vv.7-8).
7:
"Thou madest him a little lower than the angels; thou crownedst him with glory and honour, and didst set him over the works of thy hands:" The brief verse notes man made a little lower than the angels yet crowned with glory. The grammatical voices note the humbling and the honour. Barnes and Robertson note the description from the Psalm: "thou madest him a little (brachy ti — a little, in degree, or 'for a little while') lower than the angels" — man was made in rank somewhat below the angels; the phrase will be applied to Christ's temporary humiliation (v.9).

On "thou crownedst him with glory and honour," the witnesses note the dignity. Barnes and Calvin note the exaltation: "thou crownedst him with glory and honour" — God crowned man with dignity and majesty; the high rank and honour God gave to man at creation, set over the creation.

On "didst set him over the works of thy hands," the grammatical-historical note the dominion: "and didst set him over the works of thy hands" — man was given rule over the creation (Genesis 1:28); the dominion that, lost in Adam, is restored and fulfilled in Christ. The verse displays man made a little lower than the angels yet crowned with glory.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the description — "thou madest him a little (in degree, or 'for a little while') lower than the angels," man somewhat below the angels in rank, applied to Christ's humiliation (Barnes, Robertson); "thou crownedst him with glory and honour," God crowning man with dignity and majesty (Barnes, Calvin); "didst set him over the works of thy hands," man given dominion over creation (Genesis 1:28), restored in Christ (grammatical-historical). The verse displays man's dignity. The verse marks the humbling: a little lower. The verse displays the honour: crowned with glory. The verse displays the dominion: over God's works. The verse displays the application: to Christ. The description displays man honoured and given dominion. The verse displays the lowering: a little. The verse displays the crowning: with glory. The verse displays the dominion: granted. Man made a little lower than the angels yet crowned with glory displays the high destiny the Psalm ascribes to man: made somewhat below the angels, he was crowned with glory and honour and set over the works of God's hands — a dignity lost in Adam but to be fulfilled in the Son of Man. The verse displays man's crowned dominion, all things put under him (v.8).
8:
"Thou hast put all things in subjection under his feet. For in that he put all in subjection under him, he left nothing that is not put under him. But now we see not yet all things put under him." The witnesses note that all things are subjected to man in principle, though not yet seen in fact. On "thou hast put all things in subjection under his feet," the grammatical voices note the universal dominion. Barnes and Robertson note the climax of the Psalm's grant: "thou hast put all things in subjection under his feet" — the dominion given to man is universal, all things placed under him; the figure of complete subjugation.

On "he left nothing that is not put under him," the witnesses note the completeness. Barnes and Calvin note the writer's comment: "in that he put all in subjection under him, he left nothing that is not put under him" — the grant excludes nothing; the dominion is, in principle, total and without exception.

On "but now we see not yet all things put under him," the grammatical-historical note the present incompleteness: "but now we see not yet all things put under him" — in fact, man does not now exercise this universal dominion; it is not yet realized; the gap between the promise and the present is filled by Jesus (v.9), in whom the destiny is begun and guaranteed. The verse displays all things subjected to man in principle, though not yet seen in fact.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the universal dominion — "thou hast put all things in subjection under his feet," the grant total, all under man (Barnes, Robertson); "he left nothing that is not put under him," the dominion excluding nothing, total in principle (Barnes, Calvin); "but now we see not yet all things put under him," not realized in fact, the gap filled by Jesus (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dominion and its delay. The verse marks the grant: all things under his feet. The verse displays the completeness: nothing excepted. The verse displays the present: not yet seen. The verse displays the resolution: in Jesus. The subjection displays man's dominion promised but not yet realized. The verse displays the all things: subjected. The verse displays the nothing: excepted. The verse displays the not yet: present. That all things are subjected to man in principle, though not yet seen in fact, displays the tension the writer resolves: the Psalm grants man universal dominion, excepting nothing — yet we do not now see this fulfilled; the unrealized destiny is taken up and secured in Jesus, the Son of Man. The verse displays the dominion fulfilled in Jesus (v.9).
9:
"But we see Jesus, who was made a little lower than the angels for the suffering of death, crowned with glory and honour; that he by the grace of God should taste death for every man." The witnesses note Jesus crowned through death, tasting death for every man — with two cruxes. On "we see Jesus, who was made a little lower than the angels for the suffering of death, crowned with glory and honour," the grammatical voices note the fulfillment. Barnes and Robertson note that what we do not see in man we see in Jesus: he "was made a little lower than the angels for (with a view to, because of) the suffering of death" — Christ's temporary humiliation below the angels was for the sake of dying; and "crowned with glory and honour" — exalted, as a reward for his sufferings, the Psalm fulfilled in him.

On "by the grace of God," interpreters note a textual variant. Barnes, Robertson, and the grammatical-historical follow the received reading "by the grace of God (chariti theou)" — Christ tasted death by God's gracious purpose and favour; but a famous variant (known to Origen and many fathers, in two later MSS.) reads "apart from God (chōris theou)," which Ellicott and Robertson note but reject as almost certainly incorrect, though anciently widespread.

On "should taste death for every man," interpreters divide over the extent. The grammatical-historical Barnes (strongly), with Clarke and Wesley, take "taste death for every man (hyper pantos — for each and all)" as universal atonement — Christ died for all without exception; Barnes presses that the words affirm general atonement in the plainest terms. The Reformed Calvin restricts "every man" to "all the faithful" (the believers, the "man" of v.6 restored in Christ); Robertson notes the mediating formula "sufficient for all, efficient for some" (Andrew Fuller). The verse displays Jesus crowned through death, tasting death for every man.

The witnesses agree the substance: Jesus, made for a little lower than the angels for the suffering of death, is now crowned with glory and honour, having tasted death on behalf of others — the destiny man lost realized in him (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). But two cruxes divide them. On the text, "by the grace of God (chariti theou)" is the received and almost certainly correct reading, against the anciently-widespread variant "apart from God (chōris theou)" (noted by Origen, Ellicott, Robertson, but rejected). On the extent, Barnes, Clarke, and Wesley take "taste death for every man" as universal atonement (Christ died for all); Calvin restricts "every man" to the faithful; Robertson cites the formula "sufficient for all, efficient for some." The verse displays the cruxes. The verse marks the humiliation: lower for death. The verse displays the exaltation: crowned with glory. The verse displays the textual variant: by the grace, or apart from God. The verse displays the extent: for every man. The verse displays Jesus crowned through death for others. The verse displays the tasting: of death. The verse displays the grace: of God. The verse displays the every man: debated. Jesus crowned through death, tasting death for every man, displays the heart of the chapter and two cruxes: the Son, humbled below the angels to die, is crowned with glory, having by the grace of God (not, as a famous variant reads, apart from God) tasted death — for every man, read as universal atonement (Barnes, Clarke, Wesley) or for the faithful (Calvin). The verse displays the atoning death of the crowned Son, fitting for the captain of salvation (v.10).
10:
"For it became him, for whom are all things, and by whom are all things, in bringing many sons unto glory, to make the captain of their salvation perfect through sufferings." The witnesses note that it befitted God to perfect the captain of salvation through sufferings. On "it became him, for whom are all things, and by whom are all things," the grammatical voices note the fitness. Barnes and Robertson note that the plan of salvation through a suffering Saviour was fitting to God's character: "it became (was fitting, suitable to) him, for whom (the final cause) are all things, and by whom (the efficient cause) are all things" — God, the end and author of all, acted fittingly in this plan.

On "in bringing many sons unto glory," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Calvin note God's design: "in bringing many sons unto glory" — God's purpose was to lead many sons (the redeemed, made his children) to glory; the salvation aims at the glorification of many.

On "to make the captain of their salvation perfect through sufferings," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the perfecting: "to make the captain (archegon — author, leader, pioneer, the one who goes first) of their salvation perfect (teleiosai — complete, fit, consummate) through sufferings" — Christ, the leader of salvation, was made perfect (not morally, for he was sinless, but as Saviour — fully fitted, qualified, consummated as the author of salvation) through what he suffered; his sufferings perfected him for his saving office. The verse displays that it befitted God to perfect the captain of salvation through sufferings.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fitness — "it became (was fitting to) him, for whom and by whom are all things," God acting fittingly, the end and author of all (Barnes, Robertson); "in bringing many sons unto glory," God's design to lead the redeemed to glory (Barnes, Calvin); "to make the captain (author, pioneer) of their salvation perfect (fully fitted, consummated) through sufferings," Christ perfected for his saving office, not morally but as Saviour (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the perfecting. The verse marks the fitness: it became him. The verse displays the purpose: many sons to glory. The verse displays the captain: of salvation. The verse displays the means: perfect through sufferings. The fitness displays God perfecting the Saviour through suffering. The verse displays the becoming: fitting. The verse displays the sons: brought to glory. The verse displays the captain: perfected. That it befitted God to perfect the captain of salvation through sufferings displays the fitness of the plan of redemption: God, for and by whom are all things, in leading many sons to glory, fittingly made Christ, the author and leader of their salvation, perfect — fully qualified as Saviour — through what he suffered. The verse displays the perfected captain, one with his brethren (v.11).
11:
"For both he that sanctifieth and they who are sanctified are all of one: for which cause he is not ashamed to call them brethren," The witnesses note Christ and his people all of one, so he calls them brethren. On "both he that sanctifieth and they who are sanctified are all of one," the grammatical voices note the common origin. Barnes and Robertson note the ground of the brotherhood: "he that sanctifieth (Christ, who makes holy) and they who are sanctified (the believers, made holy) are all of one (ex henos — of one source, one nature, one Father, or one stock)" — Christ and his people share a common origin and nature; he is truly one with them.

On "all of one," the witnesses note the union. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ, having taken human nature, is of one nature with those he sanctifies; whether "of one" means of one Father (God), or one common humanity, the point is the real union and kinship between the Saviour and the saved.

On "he is not ashamed to call them brethren," the eastern Cyril notes the condescension: "for which cause (because of this oneness) he is not ashamed to call them brethren" — Christ, though so exalted, does not disdain to own his people as brothers; the glorious Son acknowledges the kinship, an act of wonderful condescension and love. The verse displays Christ and his people all of one, so he calls them brethren.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the common origin — "he that sanctifieth (Christ) and they who are sanctified (believers) are all of one (one source, nature, Father)," Christ truly one with his people (Barnes, Robertson); Christ of one nature with those he sanctifies, the real union and kinship (Barnes, Calvin); "he is not ashamed to call them brethren," the exalted Son owning his people as brothers, wonderful condescension (Cyril). The verse displays the brotherhood. The verse marks the oneness: all of one. The verse displays the sanctifier: Christ. The verse displays the sanctified: believers. The verse displays the kinship: brethren. The union displays Christ owning his people as brothers. The verse displays the sanctifying: Christ's. The verse displays the oneness: shared. The verse displays the brethren: owned. Christ and his people all of one, so he calls them brethren, displays the ground of the incarnation's purpose: the Sanctifier and the sanctified share one origin and nature, so that Christ, though exalted, is not ashamed to call his people brethren — owning the kinship in wonderful love. The verse displays the brotherhood of Christ and his people, declared in Scripture (vv.12-13).
12:
"Saying, I will declare thy name unto my brethren, in the midst of the church will I sing praise unto thee." The brief verse notes Christ declaring God's name among his brethren. The grammatical voices note the first proof of the brotherhood. Barnes and Robertson note the citation (Psalm 22:22, the great Messianic psalm of the cross): "I will declare thy name unto my brethren" — Christ, in the words of the Psalm, calls his people "brethren" and promises to make known God's name to them; the Saviour owns the kinship.

On "I will declare thy name," the witnesses note the revelation. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ "declares God's name (makes known the Father) unto his brethren" — the office of the incarnate Son to reveal the Father to his people; he makes God known to those he calls brothers.

On "in the midst of the church will I sing praise unto thee," the eastern Cyril notes the worship: "in the midst of the church (the congregation) will I sing praise unto thee" — Christ, in the midst of his people, leads their praise to God; he is one with them in worship, the chief singer in the assembly of the brethren. The verse displays Christ declaring God's name among his brethren.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the citation (Psalm 22:22, the psalm of the cross) — "I will declare thy name unto my brethren," Christ calling his people brethren and promising to reveal God's name (Barnes, Robertson); "declare thy name," the incarnate Son's office to make known the Father (Barnes, Calvin); "in the midst of the church will I sing praise," Christ leading his people's praise, one with them in worship (Cyril). The verse displays the brotherhood proved. The verse marks the declaring: thy name. The verse displays the brethren: his people. The verse displays the revelation: making God known. The verse displays the worship: in the church. The citation displays Christ owning and leading his brethren. The verse displays the name: declared. The verse displays the brethren: owned. The verse displays the praise: led. Christ declaring God's name among his brethren displays the first scriptural proof of the brotherhood: in the words of the psalm of the cross, Christ calls his people brethren, promises to make known God's name to them, and sings God's praise in the midst of the congregation — one with his people. The verse displays the brotherhood proved, with further testimony (v.13).
13:
"And again, I will put my trust in him. And again, Behold I and the children which God hath given me." The brief verse notes Christ's trust and his children, proving his brotherhood with men. On "I will put my trust in him," the grammatical voices note the shared faith. Barnes and Robertson note the second and third citations (Isaiah 8:17-18): "I will put my trust in him" — Christ, as man, exercises trust in God, sharing the believer's posture of faith; he is one with his brethren in depending on God.

On "I will put my trust in him," the witnesses note the human likeness. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ's trusting in God shows his true humanity and his union with his people; he stands with them as one who, in his human nature, leans on God; the Saviour shares the life of faith.

On "Behold I and the children which God hath given me," the eastern Cyril notes the family: "Behold I and the children which God hath given me" — Christ presents himself with the "children" God gave him (the redeemed), as a family; he and his people are one household, the children entrusted to him by the Father. The verse displays Christ's trust and his children, proving his brotherhood with men.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the citations (Isaiah 8:17-18) — "I will put my trust in him," Christ as man trusting God, sharing the believer's faith (Barnes, Robertson); his trusting showing his true humanity and union with his people, sharing the life of faith (Barnes, Calvin); "Behold I and the children which God hath given me," Christ with the redeemed as a family, one household (Cyril). The verse displays the proofs. The verse marks the trust: in him. The verse displays the humanity: Christ leaning on God. The verse displays the children: given him. The verse displays the family: one household. The citations display Christ one with his people in faith and family. The verse displays the trusting: shared. The verse displays the children: God's gift. The verse displays the household: one. Christ's trust and his children, proving his brotherhood with men, display the further scriptural witness: Christ, in the prophet's words, puts his trust in God as his brethren do, and presents himself with the children God gave him — sharing both their faith and their family, one with them as man. The verse displays the kinship proved, grounding the incarnation (v.14).
14:
"Forasmuch then as the children are partakers of flesh and blood, he also himself likewise took part of the same; that through death he might destroy him that had the power of death, that is, the devil;" The witnesses note Christ sharing flesh and blood to destroy the devil through death. On "as the children are partakers of flesh and blood, he also himself likewise took part of the same," the grammatical voices note the incarnation. Barnes and Robertson note the reason for the incarnation: "as the children (those he came to save) are partakers of (share in) flesh and blood (human nature), he also himself likewise took part of (shared, partook of) the same" — because his people are human, the Son himself took true human nature; the incarnation grounded in his union with the children.

On "that through death he might destroy him that had the power of death," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Calvin note the design: "that through death (his own dying) he might destroy (katargese — bring to nought, render powerless, annul) him that had the power (the dominion, the sway) of death" — by dying, Christ broke the power of the one who wielded death; the means of victory was death itself.

On "that is, the devil," the eastern Cyril notes the enemy: the one destroyed is "the devil" — Satan, who through sin and death held men in his grip; Christ's death broke Satan's power over death, defeating the enemy by entering and conquering his own domain. The verse displays Christ sharing flesh and blood to destroy the devil through death.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the incarnation — "as the children are partakers of flesh and blood (human nature), he also himself took part of the same," the Son taking true humanity because his people are human (Barnes, Robertson); "that through death he might destroy (render powerless) him that had the power of death," Christ breaking death's power by his own dying (Barnes, Calvin); "that is, the devil," Satan who held men through death, defeated by Christ in his own domain (Cyril). The verse displays the incarnation's purpose. The verse marks the sharing: flesh and blood. The verse displays the incarnation: he took the same. The verse displays the means: through death. The verse displays the victory: destroyed the devil. The incarnation displays Christ taking humanity to conquer death and Satan. The verse displays the flesh and blood: shared. The verse displays the death: the means. The verse displays the devil: destroyed. Christ sharing flesh and blood to destroy the devil through death displays the purpose of the incarnation: because his people share flesh and blood, the Son took the same — that through his own death he might break the power of the devil, who held the dominion of death. The verse displays the conquest of the devil, delivering the enslaved (v.15).
15:
"And deliver them who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage." The brief verse notes Christ delivering those enslaved by the fear of death. The grammatical voices note the deliverance. Barnes and Robertson note the further purpose of Christ's victory: "and deliver (set free, release) them who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage (douleias — slavery)" — Christ frees those whom the dread of death had held in lifelong slavery.

On "through fear of death," the witnesses note the bondage. Barnes and Calvin note the cruel slavery: the "fear of death" — the terror of dying, with its dread of judgment and the unknown — held men in bondage all their lives; this fear is a tyranny from which men cannot free themselves.

On "deliver them," the grammatical-historical note the liberation: by destroying the power of death and the devil (v.14), Christ delivers his people from this fear; the believer, knowing death conquered, is freed from its terror and its lifelong bondage; deliverance from the fear of death is a fruit of the atonement. The verse displays Christ delivering those enslaved by the fear of death.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the deliverance — "deliver (set free) them who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage (slavery)," Christ freeing those held in lifelong slavery by dread of death (Barnes, Robertson); the "fear of death," the terror of dying and judgment, a tyranny men cannot escape (Barnes, Calvin); by conquering death and the devil, Christ frees his people from this fear, a fruit of the atonement (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the deliverance. The verse marks the freeing: deliver them. The verse displays the bondage: fear of death. The verse displays the duration: all their lifetime. The verse displays the means: Christ's victory. The deliverance displays the enslaved freed from death's terror. The verse displays the fear: of death. The verse displays the bondage: lifelong. The verse displays the deliverance: by Christ. Christ delivering those enslaved by the fear of death displays the gracious fruit of his conquest: by breaking the power of death and the devil, he sets free those whom the dread of death had held in lifelong bondage — the believer, knowing death conquered, is delivered from its terror. The verse displays the freedom from the fear of death, grounded in Christ's taking human nature (v.16).
16:
"For verily he took not on him the nature of angels; but he took on him the seed of Abraham." The witnesses note that Christ took human nature, not angelic — with the rendering crux. On the meaning of the verse, interpreters divide over the verb. The Reformed Gill and Calvin follow the received rendering "he took not on him the nature of angels; but... the seed of Abraham" — Christ assumed human nature, not the nature of angels; Calvin presses this against Marcion and the docetists, that Christ was real man, of human seed. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson, with the eastern Chrysostom and wesleyan-arminian Clarke, prefer the marginal reading: "he taketh not hold of (epilambanetai) angels, but he taketh hold of the seed of Abraham" — the verb meaning to lay hold of in order to help, rescue, or succour; Christ lays hold of fallen man to save him, not the fallen angels. Chrysostom: as man fled, Christ pursued and laid hold of him; Clarke: Christ caught the falling human creature, but let the angels fall.

On "the seed of Abraham," the witnesses note the objects. Barnes and Ellicott note that Christ lays hold of (or takes the nature of) "the seed of Abraham" — whether the literal descendants (fitting for Jewish readers) or, more broadly, the spiritual seed, the children of faith (Galatians 3:29); the help and the kinship are to Abraham's seed.

The witnesses note the kindness either way: whether "took the nature of" or "took hold of," the verse magnifies God's love to man over angels. The verse displays that Christ took human nature, not angelic.

The witnesses agree the substance: Christ's saving work is for man, not the fallen angels — he is concerned with the seed of Abraham, magnifying God's love to the human race over the angels (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Chrysostom). But the rendering divides them: Gill and Calvin follow "he took not on him the nature of angels but the seed of Abraham" (Christ assumed human, not angelic, nature, against the docetists); Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson, Chrysostom, and Clarke prefer "he taketh hold not of angels but of the seed of Abraham" (the verb meaning to lay hold of to help and rescue, Christ seizing fallen man to save him). The verse displays the rendering crux. The verse marks the negative: not angels. The verse displays the debate: took nature, or took hold to help. The verse displays the object: the seed of Abraham. The verse displays the love: to man over angels. The verse displays Christ saving man, not the fallen angels. The verse displays the nature: human. The verse displays the taking hold: to rescue. The verse displays the seed: of Abraham. That Christ took human nature, not angelic, displays the wonder of his incarnation and a rendering crux: he is concerned not with angels but with the seed of Abraham — read as "he took not on him the nature of angels but of man" (Gill, Calvin) or "he taketh hold, to save, not of angels but of the seed of Abraham" (Barnes, Ellicott, Chrysostom, Clarke). The verse displays the love that took hold of man, requiring full likeness to the brethren (v.17).
17:
"Wherefore in all things it behoved him to be made like unto his brethren, that he might be a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God, to make reconciliation for the sins of the people." The witnesses note Christ made like his brethren to be a merciful and faithful high priest. On "in all things it behoved him to be made like unto his brethren," the grammatical voices note the necessity. Barnes and Robertson note the conclusion: "in all things (in every respect, except sin) it behoved (was necessary, fitting) him to be made like unto his brethren" — Christ had to be fully human, like those he came to save, in all things; the incarnation was a necessity for his saving office.

On "that he might be a merciful and faithful high priest," the witnesses note the priesthood introduced. Barnes and Calvin note the great theme now opened: "that he might be a merciful (compassionate, sympathizing) and faithful (trustworthy, reliable) high priest in things pertaining to God" — Christ's full humanity fitted him to be the high priest, merciful toward men and faithful toward God; the first mention of the priesthood that is the epistle's grand subject.

On "to make reconciliation for the sins of the people," the grammatical-historical note the priestly work: "to make reconciliation for (hilaskesthai — to make propitiation, atonement for) the sins of the people" — the high priest's office is to atone for sin; Christ, the merciful and faithful priest, makes propitiation for the people's sins. The verse displays Christ made like his brethren to be a merciful and faithful high priest.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the necessity — "in all things (except sin) it behoved (was necessary) him to be made like unto his brethren," Christ fully human like those he saves (Barnes, Robertson); "a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God," his humanity fitting him to be the priest, compassionate to men and faithful to God, the epistle's grand theme opened (Barnes, Calvin); "to make reconciliation (propitiation, atonement) for the sins of the people," the priestly work of atoning for sin (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the high priest. The verse marks the likeness: like his brethren. The verse displays the office: merciful and faithful high priest. The verse displays the realm: things pertaining to God. The verse displays the work: reconciliation for sins. The conclusion displays Christ fitted as the atoning high priest. The verse displays the likeness: full. The verse displays the priest: merciful. The verse displays the atonement: made. Christ made like his brethren to be a merciful and faithful high priest displays the purpose of the incarnation now fully unfolded: in all things he had to be made like his brethren, that he might be a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God, to make propitiation for the sins of the people. The verse displays the atoning high priest, able to succour the tempted (v.18).
18:
"For in that he himself hath suffered being tempted, he is able to succour them that are tempted." The witnesses note that the tempted Christ is able to succour the tempted. On "in that he himself hath suffered being tempted," the grammatical voices note the shared experience. Barnes and Robertson note the ground of Christ's sympathy: "in that he himself hath suffered being tempted (having been tried, tested, and suffered in his testing)" — Christ, in his humanity, really suffered under temptation and trial; he knows by experience what it is to be tempted.

On "being tempted," the witnesses note the reality. Barnes and Calvin note that Christ's temptations were real — he faced genuine trial and suffering (in the wilderness, in Gethsemane, throughout his life); his sympathy is not theoretical but founded on his own experience of suffering under temptation.

On "he is able to succour them that are tempted," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the comfort: "he is able to succour (boethesai — help, run to the aid of, bring timely relief to) them that are tempted" — because he has felt temptation, he can sympathize with and help his people in theirs; the tempted Saviour is the able helper of the tempted. The verse displays that the tempted Christ is able to succour the tempted.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ground of sympathy — "in that he himself hath suffered being tempted (tried, tested, suffering in his testing)," Christ really suffering under temptation, knowing it by experience (Barnes, Robertson); his temptations real, his sympathy founded on his own suffering (Barnes, Calvin); "he is able to succour (help, run to the aid of) them that are tempted," the tempted Saviour the able helper of the tempted (Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the able Saviour. The verse marks the experience: suffered being tempted. The verse displays the reality: real temptation. The verse displays the ability: to succour. The verse displays the objects: the tempted. The experience displays Christ helping the tempted from his own suffering. The verse displays the temptation: suffered. The verse displays the succour: given. The verse displays the tempted: helped. That the tempted Christ is able to succour the tempted displays the comfort that closes the chapter: because Christ himself really suffered under temptation, he can sympathize with and bring timely help to his people when they are tempted — the tested Saviour is the able helper of the tested. The verse displays the sympathizing high priest, who closes the great chapter on the Son made man for our salvation.
